title: 2025 11 13 Petitions
displayTitle: 2025-11-13 - Petitions Committee - Local Government Professionals NZ
description: Petition of Melinda McGregor: Councils need clear guidelines of required actions following a dog attack Taituarā | Local Government Professionals NZ Raymond Horan, Chief Advisor Philip Shackleton, Principal Advisor Billy Charlton, Environmental Services Manager, Waimakariri District Council Lionel Bridger, Manager Animal Services, Christchurch City Council
descriptionSummary: Local Government Professionals NZ. Raymond Horan, Chief Advisor. Philip Shackleton, Principal Advisor

chunks.text: Speak to some of the more technical aspects of this matter. Alright, well look, I'm, I'm Greg O'Connor, the chair of the committee, and we have on my right, uh, Greg Flemming for Mango here in uh, Alton. Um, I am Pao Garcia off to the right of the screen. Um, MP for Newly And Kda. I'm Ka Carter.

Um, green Party List MP based in Christchurch. Alright, welcome. Um, Tata, I got that right, Tata, that's the pronunciation. Good. Um, now 10 minutes, uh, take your submissions read, but we're keen to hear from, uh, any additional, um, submissions on points and hopefully leave time for questions.

Okay, thank you. So Koto Koto, uh, thank you for the opportunity to speak, uh, to this important, uh, petition Co. Phillip Shackleton ua. Uh, I'm the principal advisor of Sector Readiness at t I'm joined today by Raymond Horan, who is our chief advisor and also line with us is Lionel Bridger, who is the manager, animal Services from Christchurch City Council.

And Billy Charlton, who's the Environment Services Manager from Wey Mac Area District Council. First, we want to acknowledge the seriousness of dog attacks and the lasting impact they have on victims, families and communities. We also acknowledge the petitioner's commitment to improving public safety and victim support.

We'd like to highlight three points from our submission. Firstly, councils under the Dog Control Act 1996 operate within strict legal boundaries. Dogs are legally considered property and euthanasia of a dog generally requires a court order.

Every decision to prosecute following a dog attack must meet evidentiary standards and follow the solicitor general's pro prosecution guidelines. For example, in the Y Macari case, the council applied the legal requirements under the act. The council classified the dog as dangerous under section 31 and under section 32 the dog was required to be muted with conditions imposed on the owner that removed any further risk to the public.

As a result, the dog has not reoffended. Secondly, we do not support a mandatory national dog bite scale under the Dog Control Act 1996. While tools like the Dunbar Scale can help mandating one risks limiting enforcement discretion, councils need the ability to investigate dog attacks on a case by case basis.

And this allows councils to take into consideration broad factors such as the dog's history, compliance, record, and community risk. And thirdly, the DO Control Act is nearly 30 years old. The current system is generally fit for purpose, but we believe a review would be beneficial.

We support a sector-wide consultation before any legislative changes. We do not support penalties for councils. These could undermine discretion and lead to risk averse enforcement instead will be back DIA's approach of strengthening community consultation for bylaws.

Looking ahead, we recently attended the launch of the New Zealand Animal Law Association's report on dog attacks, which opens a valuable con conversation about updating the act. DIA has already started reviewing enforcement guidelines and we've helped circulate their survey to animal control managers to ensure wide engagement across the sector.

In short, public safety matters, enforcement must remain fair and legally sound collaboration is key to any future changes. Thank you again for the opportunity to speak. We are happy to take any questions and our colleagues online can provide further detail.

Uh, we have the opinion that we need to assess the history of the dog in relation to aggressive behavior and our experience.

The majority of dog bites, um, are deescalated through human inter intervention. Uh, people get involved and they stop the dogs from fighting resulting in injuries that would be considered on levels one to three on a scale because the, um, people intervened and mitigated any serious injuries.

However, that dog has demonstrated a potentially to be aggressive. Um, we must have the ability to apply the classifications of dogs as menacing requiring muzzling in public place. And we also need to have the ability to continue to classify dogs as dangerous, which places additional responsibility on the owners.

Um, we also have the view that in whether a dog is demonstrated, um, a history of aggressive behavior, um, we must be able to prosecute those owners who demonstrate there's no willingness to comply with the provisions of the act. Um, and we've recently had a pro successful prosecution in Christchurch where the substantive dog attack on a domestic animal was relatively minor, but the dog had a significant history of demonstrating aggressive behavior.

And if we had applied, uh, scale similar to Dr. Dunbar, that would've limited our ability to actually initiate a prosecution, um, where this dog posed a significant public safety risk. Mm-hmm.
